text: The surgeon begins creating a flap on the left side, just outside the preperitoneal fat pad, using three eight-millimeter ports. The dissection follows parallel to the preperitoneal fat pad, utilizing blunt dissection to separate the tissue layers.

The surgeon begins creating a flap on the left side, using three eight-millimeter ports. The dissection follows parallel to the preperitoneal fat pad, utilizing blunt dissection techniques to separate the tissue within the fat pad.

The surgeon dissects superiorly and inferiorly around the hernia defect, maintaining the fat layer down and the white posterior sheath up to avoid perforations. The flap is extended further inferiorly, crossing the midline below the hernia defect. Scissor dissection is used as the fat adheres more closely to the posterior sheath near the epigastric hernia. The dissection continues above and below the hernia defect, sometimes reaching behind it, before starting the reduction.

The surgeon dissects superiorly and inferiorly around the hernia defect, maintaining the preperitoneal fat below and the white posterior sheath above. The flap is extended further inferiorly, crossing the midline below the hernia defect. Scissor dissection is used to separate adherent fat from the posterior sheath as the epigastric hernia is reached. Dissection continues above and below the hernia defect, aiming to reduce it.

The surgeon reduces the primary hernia, revealing a bilobed defect with a bridge of fascia and a large wad of pre-peritoneal fat and phosphor ligaments protruding into the hernia defect. The small neck of the hernia is visible alongside the large wad of tissue.

The surgeon reduces the primary hernia, which appears bilobed with a bridge of fascia across it. A large wad of pre-peritoneal fat and phosphor ligaments is visible within the hernia defect, contributing to the patient's symptoms. The hernia has a small neck and a significant amount of tissue protruding.

The surgeon continues dissection to the contralateral side, closely following the posterior sheath within the preperitoneal fat pad. The dissection is facilitated by the ease of working within the fat pad, and the flap is extended further on the contralateral side to accommodate a 15x15 mesh for a durable repair.

The surgeon continues the dissection towards the contralateral side, closely following the posterior sheath within the preperitoneal fat pad. The dissection is facilitated by the ease of working within the fat pad, and the flap is extended further on the contralateral side to accommodate a 15x15 mesh for a durable repair.

The surgeon dissects down towards the umbilicus, uncovering a small hernia defect with incarcerated fat. Umbilical ligaments are visible entering the umbilicus, which are divided to facilitate the dissection. The dissection continues inferiorly to create sufficient space for the placement of a larger mesh.

The surgeon dissects down towards the umbilicus, uncovering a small hernia defect with incarcerated fat. Umbilical ligaments are visible and divided. The dissection continues inferiorly to create space for mesh coverage, extending laterally to the contralateral side using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection with cautery.

The surgeon continues lateral dissection towards the contralateral side using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection with cautery. The dissection is performed carefully to avoid creating holes in the thin tissue, aiming to create a wide pocket larger than fifteen by fifteen centimeters for mesh placement.

The surgeon performs a dissection in the preperitoneal plane, carefully avoiding the creation of holes in the thin tissue. Using blunt dissection and focused cautery, the surgeon works to create a wide pocket slightly larger than fifteen by fifteen centimeters for mesh placement.

The surgeon measures the pocket created for mesh placement, confirming its size. The heavyweight mesh, measuring fifteen by fifteen centimeters, is positioned off-center due to the narrow creation on the ipsilateral side. The peritoneal flap is extended across the viscera, ensuring closure past the midline.

The surgeon extends the dissection far to the contralateral side, ensuring the peritoneal flap lays across the viscera. The pocket is measured to confirm its size, accommodating a fifteen by fifteen mesh, which will not be centered due to the narrow creation on the ipsilateral side.

The surgeon selects a heavyweight mesh due to the patient's body habitus and proceeds to sew the hernia defect closed, ensuring not to penetrate the skin. The inverted pseudosec dermis is imbricated, and the pressure is reduced to ten during closure. The same suture is used to close a small umbilical hernia defect, which will be covered by a large mesh with good overlap.

The surgeon closes the hernia defect using sutures, imbricating the inverted pseudosec dermis while ensuring the suture does not penetrate the skin. The pressure is reduced during closure, and the same suture is used to close a small umbilical hernia defect, which will be covered by a large piece of mesh.

The surgeon places a large mesh over the hernia defect, using a 3-0 silk suture to secure the mesh at its center and through the defect. Additional tacking sutures are placed at the corners to prevent rolling, including on the ipsilateral side. The mesh is centered and held in place while the peritoneal flap is sutured closed.

The surgeon places a large mesh over the hernia defect, using a 3-0 silk suture to secure it. The suture is threaded through the center of the mesh, passed through the main hernia defect, and then back through the mesh, ensuring the mesh is centered and tied in place.

The surgeon places tacking sutures in the corners of the mesh to prevent rolling, starting with the far right side superiorly, then the right inferior corner, and finally on the ipsilateral side to hold it up. The flap is closed over the mesh, with sutures going through the fat pad to enhance strength and prevent tearing.

The surgeon adjusts the positioning of the ports, noting the proximity to the surgical site. The peritoneal flap is pulled beyond the mesh with ease due to its laxity, ensuring complete coverage. Sutures are placed through the fat pad to enhance the strength of the flap closure, minimizing the risk of tearing.
The surgeon uses a V-lock suture to close a small hole in the mesh, providing a final view of the inverted sac before concluding the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon uses a V-lock suture to close a small hole in the surgical area. The inverted sac is visible, marking the completion of the hernia repair procedure.
